details.text: Derivative: صَدْرَ (chest) is specified as it is a place which can feel tightened and constricted by difficulties and heavy burdens.

Derivative: نَشْرَحْ is from the word شَرَحَ – شَرْحًا. In contemporary Arabic شَرْح (Sharh) is used to mean explanation or expounding. It can be understood to mean: To open up the meaning of something that was otherwise complex. Ibn Faris said that there are two fundamental components to the meaning of the word شَرْح (Sharh). The first is to open or expand something. The second is to clarify something.

أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ

- When a question is presented with a negation like in the statement أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ “Have We not opened ...?” then its purpose if for affirmation and it implies: “For sure I have opened …”. The rhetorical form of questioning is used to remind of a favour that had been given. It is to remind the one who was given a favour to be grateful and appreciative.
- There is a saying in Arabic that states: شَرَحَ اللهُ لَهُ صَدْرَه which literally means: “Allah opened or expanded his chest”. It can imply that “Allah has put his mind at ease” in the sense that the Messenger’s ﷺ chest (or heart and mind) has been filled with contentment. The شَرْح (Sharh) of the chest can also imply: “That any impediments, barriers or constraints have been removed leaving the chest in a state of contentment and comfort that allows it to accept something with ease and clarity” i.e., it refers to the preparation of the Messenger ﷺ for the receiving of the Message and for his understanding of it. The statement would therefore mean: “Has Allah not opened or expanded your chest (by removing all barriers) so that you can receive and understand the revelation with clarity”.
- By advancing لَكَ (For you) in the sentence structure, it implies اِخْتِصاص (Ikhtisaas – A special specification) and the statement would therefore mean: “Did We not open your chest especially for you” i.e., this expansion of the chest was unique to him and special for him.

Derivative: وَضَعْنَا from وَضَعَ means to place something down on something. It is also used for when a mother gives birth to a child with the meaning of dropping a burden. وَضَعَ with عَن means to take something off, to remove or to alleviate and relieve.
Derivative: وِزْرَ refers to an intense or heavy burden or weight that is carried. It refers to a burden that can crush you under its weight.

وَوَضَعْنَا عَنْكَ وِزْرَكَ

- “And We have relieved (وَوَضَعْنَا) your burden (وِزْرَكَ) from you (عَنْكَ)”. The advancement of عَنْكَ “from you” in the sentence structure indicates that this is special to the Messenger ﷺ and a favour to him especially.

Derivative: أَنْقَضَ – نَقَضَ – نَقْض can mean to break or demolish, to untie or undo, to violate or break (i.e., a contract, treaty or agreement). أَنْقضَ – إِنْقَاض can mean to place such a burden on something or someone that you can start hearing breaking and creaking which indicates to an impending collapse. In short in means to weigh heavily to the verge of collapse or breaking.

وَرَفَعْنَا لَكَ ذِكْرَكَ

- “And we have elevated (وَرَفَعْنَا) for you (لَكَ) your mention (ذِكْرَكَ)”. The advancement of لَكَ “for you” in the sentence structure indicates that this is special to the Messenger ﷺ and a favour to him especially.
Derivative: الْعُسْرِ refers to a difficulty or a time of difficulty.
Derivative: يُسْرًا refers to something that is easier and smooth i.e., that which has no difficulty.
Derivative: مَعَ (with) combines two things or matters and puts two things in the company of each other; either simultaneous or one straight after the other but always together.

فَإِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْرًا

- الْعُسْرِ is مَعْرِفَة (Ma’rifah i.e., defined) preceded by ال (Alif Laam) whilst يُسْرًا is نَكِرَة (Nakirah i.e., undefined) with تَنْوِين (Tanween – ً). This indicates that the ease that accompanies the difficulty is more and greater.

إِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْرًا

- The repetition of this statement here is for reiteration and reassurance.

Derivative: انْصَبْ is the command form from نَصَبَ – نَصَبٌ which means to exert and tire oneself for something. Here it refers to exerting and exhausting oneself in the worship of Allah in prayer during the night.
Derivative: فَرَغْتَ from فَرَغَ which means to be free and finished from the acts, tasks and activities that keep a person busy and occupied.

Derivative: ارْغَبْ is the command form from رَغِبَ – يَرْغَبُ – رَغْبَةٌ which when followed by إِلَى (Ilaa - to or towards) means to covet and long for something i.e., to direct or turn oneself with longing and craving towards.
